name: Hurricane Knob
short_description: High-elevation rockhounding site on the Blue Ridge Parkway near Stuart. Hunt for quartz crystals and feldspar specimens in the Appalachian highlands.
description:
Hurricane Knob sits at 3045 feet elevation within the scenic Blue Ridge Parkway corridor in Patrick County. This site offers rockhounders access to the ancient metamorphic terrain that characterizes Virginia's Blue Ridge mountains, where collectors can search for quartz specimens, feldspar crystals, and mica fragments along road cuts and natural outcrops.

The gentle slopes and forested terrain of this high-elevation location make it suitable for rockhounds seeking Appalachian mineral specimens. The area has been shaped by Precambrian metamorphic activity and later weathering, exposing mineral-bearing rock formations typical of the Blue Ridge physiographic province. Visitors should note that the Blue Ridge Parkway has specific regulations and limited collection opportunities compared to open BLM lands.

Access is from the scenic parkway itself, which offers pullouts and overlooks. The elevation provides cooler temperatures and pleasant conditions during warmer months. Best results come from examining road cuts where fresh rock faces are exposed, though collecting must follow all National Park Service guidelines. The site is ideal for rockhounds interested in Appalachian geology and mineral sampling.
history: Hurricane Knob's location within the Blue Ridge Mountains places it in terrain shaped by Precambrian metamorphic activity around 1 billion years ago, when continental collision created the Grenville orogeny. The exposed metamorphic rocks, primarily schist and gneiss, have been weathered and fractured over millions of years, releasing quartz and feldspar crystals that can be found in road cuts and surface exposures along the parkway.
tips: Focus your search on exposed road cuts and natural rock outcrops where fresh surfaces reveal quartz veins and feldspar crystals. Bring a magnifying glass to identify feldspar phenocrysts and mica sheets within metamorphic rock faces. Look for areas where weathering has loosened mineral specimens, particularly in soil patches near exposed bedrock. Check the parkway's current collection guidelines before visiting, as regulations may restrict removal of specimens depending on the specific area and time of year.
terrain_notes: Elevation of 3045 feet with gentle slopes and forested terrain typical of the Blue Ridge. Hiking distance from parking pullouts to productive areas ranges from short walks to moderate 0.5-1 mile explorations. Footing is generally stable on maintained parkway access areas, though natural terrain beyond pullouts can be uneven with roots, leaf litter, and loose rock. Summer temperatures are cooler than surrounding lowlands due to elevation.
best_season: May - September. Higher elevation provides relief from summer heat, and spring through early fall offers the most pleasant weather for extended rockhounding sessions. Roads are clear and accessible during this period. Winter conditions can include snow and ice at this elevation, making access difficult.
written_directions: From Stuart, Virginia, take Parkway Drive northwest toward the Blue Ridge Parkway entrance. Enter the Blue Ridge Parkway and follow it northward for approximately 8-10 miles. Hurricane Knob area is accessible via pullouts and overlooks along the parkway near milepost 216-220. Look for designated scenic pullouts where you can safely park and access exposed rock formations. The exact access point may vary; consult current parkway maps for specific pullout locations.
road_conditions: Well-maintained parkway road with good surface conditions. Scenic drive, no high-clearance vehicles required. Access limited to designated parkway pullouts.
parking_notes: Scenic pullouts and overlooks along the Blue Ridge Parkway provide parking. Limited spaces at individual pullouts. Parking is free but must be in designated areas only.
collection_rules: Collection regulations on the Blue Ridge Parkway are strict. Personal use collection only where specifically permitted. No removal of specimens from protected areas or historic sites. No motorized equipment. Maximum reasonable amount for personal collection. Check with Blue Ridge Parkway Visitor Center before collecting to confirm current policies for this specific area. No commercial collecting allowed.
quantity_limits: Reasonable personal use amounts only; verify current limits with parkway management before collecting.
rules:
Personal use collection only in designated areas
No commercial collection
No motorized equipment
No removal from protected or historic areas
Check current parkway regulations before visiting
All specimens collected must be for personal use only
nearest_services: Stuart, Virginia, approximately 8-10 miles south. Limited services directly along the Blue Ridge Parkway. Plan to visit nearby towns for gas, food, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible from designated parkway pullouts and overlooks. Easy walking distances from parking areas. Well-maintained parkway infrastructure. Not wheelchair accessible for natural collecting areas, but scenic overlooks and visitor facilities are accessible. Suitable for families with young children and seniors seeking easy access.

faq.question: What gems and minerals can I find rockhounding at Hurricane Knob on the Blue Ridge Parkway?
faq.answer: Hurricane Knob is known for quartz crystals, feldspar specimens, and mica fragments typical of the Appalachian Blue Ridge metamorphic terrain. The site's elevation and exposed road cuts provide opportunities to find these minerals weathered from ancient metamorphic rock formations.

faq.question: Is there a fee to rockhound at Hurricane Knob, and what are the collection rules?
faq.answer: There is no rockhounding fee at Hurricane Knob, but strict Blue Ridge Parkway regulations apply. Personal use collection only is allowed in designated areas. No commercial collecting, motorized equipment, or removal from protected sites permitted. Always check current parkway policies before visiting.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Stuart, Virginia at Hurricane Knob?
faq.answer: May through September is optimal for visiting Hurricane Knob. The higher elevation (3045 feet) provides cooler temperatures than surrounding areas, and roads remain clear and accessible. Winter conditions can bring snow and ice at this elevation, making access difficult.

faq.question: How do I get to Hurricane Knob from Stuart, and what kind of vehicle do I need?
faq.answer: From Stuart, take Parkway Drive northwest to the Blue Ridge Parkway entrance and drive north approximately 8-10 miles. A standard passenger vehicle is sufficient on the well-maintained parkway road. Park only in designated scenic pullouts and overlooks along the route.

faq.question: Is Hurricane Knob rockhounding site beginner-friendly and suitable for families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Hurricane Knob is beginner-friendly and family-appropriate. Easy access from parkway pullouts means minimal hiking required, and the terrain is relatively stable. Children ages 6 and up can comfortably explore with supervision. The scenic setting makes it an enjoyable outing for all skill levels.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring rockhounding at Hurricane Knob on the Blue Ridge Parkway?
faq.answer: Bring a small magnifying glass to identify feldspar and mica within rock specimens, a rock hammer for responsible collecting, sturdy hiking boots, water, and sun protection. The area offers limited services, so plan supplies in advance. Check current parkway collection guidelines before packing tools.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Hurricane Knob on Virginia's Blue Ridge Parkway. Easy public rockhounding near Stuart with scenic mountain views.
